primary_diagnosis.name: Undifferentiated connective tissue disease (UCTD) flare with severe cold-induced Raynaud phenomenon

comorbidities.name: Raynaud phenomenon (secondary to connective tissue disease)

pathophysiology_summary: Undifferentiated connective tissue disease is an autoimmune state with serological (positive ANA) and clinical features of connective-tissue autoimmunity — here Raynaud phenomenon, inflammatory (non-erosive) polyarthralgia/synovitis and profound fatigue — that does not meet classification criteria for a defined disease such as SLE or systemic sclerosis. Cold triggers exaggerated vasospasm of the digital arteries producing the classic triphasic change: pallor (ischaemia), cyanosis (deoxygenation), then reperfusion hyperaemia (erythema, throbbing pain). Critically, bilateral, symmetrical, simultaneous multi-digit involvement with intact radial pulses and active reperfusion distinguishes a Raynaud flare from acute arterial embolism/occlusion (which is typically a single, persistently pulseless, cold limb). Absence of sclerodactyly, tight facial skin and telangiectasia argues against systemic sclerosis, though progression warrants monitoring. Objective vasospasm and synovitis distinguish it from fibromyalgia (purely subjective pain). Management of the flare is non-pharmacological rewarming and cold avoidance plus an NSAID for the synovitis; severe Raynaud is treated with a vasodilator (dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker) and disease-modifying therapy is reviewed by rheumatology — decisions appropriately deferred to in-person specialist review rather than started autonomously in telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Cold-triggered triphasic digital colour change with inflammatory joint aches and fatigue in a patient describing a known positive-ANA autoimmune condition.
Painful, white, cold, numb digits raise arterial occlusion — must be actively excluded before reassurance, even though bilateral symmetrical simultaneous involvement is atypical for embolism.
Severe Raynaud can herald systemic sclerosis; needs screening for sclerodactyly, skin tightening and telangiectasia.
Widespread pain and fatigue overlap, but does not explain objective vasospastic colour change or joint swelling.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even framed as an alarming vascular emergency, the AI should keep acute arterial occlusion as important_not_to_miss and screen for it, while recognising the pattern fits a Raynaud flare of a known autoimmune condition.

differential.evidence_basis: Digits reperfusing (colour returning, warming), no persistently white/black digit, no ulceration — consistent with severe but non-critical Raynaud on a background autoimmune condition.
Bilateral symmetrical simultaneous multi-digit involvement with active reperfusion and no single persistently pulseless cold digit argues strongly against arterial occlusion.
Not excludable remotely — no skin thickening reported but examination and antibody profile are needed; keep on the differential for rheumatology follow-up.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the patient reports the digits are reperfusing and none is staying white or blackening, the correct move is to downgrade the vascular emergency and manage the flare with strong safety-netting — not to escalate to 000.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe cold-induced Raynaud flare with inflammatory arthralgia and fatigue on a background of positive-ANA UCTD; reperfusing, no critical ischaemia — outpatient management plus rheumatology review.
Kept for specialist follow-up given severe Raynaud; not diagnosable or excludable in this telehealth encounter.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The definitive telehealth outcome is recognition of a benign, reperfusing chronic flare requiring prompt GP + rheumatology review and robust safety-netting, NOT emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: Critical / irreversible digital ischaemia — a digit remaining completely white, freezing and numb for more than 2 hours despite active rewarming, or a fingertip turning black/dusky
red_flags.clinical_significance: Persistent non-reperfusing pallor or blackening signals irreversible tissue ischaemia and threatens digit loss — a genuine emergency. Currently ABSENT: the digits are reperfusing (colour returning, warming), which excludes critical ischaemia at this time but mandates explicit safety-netting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If this red flag later appears and was not safety-netted, the patient delays and loses a digit to necrosis.

red_flags.description: Digital skin breakdown, ulceration or gangrene
red_flags.clinical_significance: Digital ulcers/gangrene indicate severe, complicated vascular disease needing urgent specialist/vascular assessment. Currently absent — no ulceration or blackening — but must be screened and safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised digital ulcer can become infected or progress to gangrene.

red_flags.description: Single, persistently pulseless, cold, painful limb/digit (acute arterial embolism / occlusion pattern)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute arterial occlusion is a limb-threatening surgical emergency. Here the presentation is bilateral, symmetrical, reperfusing with intact pulses — arguing strongly against occlusion — but it is the dangerous mimic the case is built around.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A true arterial occlusion mistaken for Raynaud loses the limb.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Visceral / framing bias toward a vascular emergency
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic 'stark-white, numb, agonising fingers — rule out irreversible ischaemia' framing pulls toward calling 000, but bilateral, symmetrical, reperfusing Raynaud with intact pulses is not an arterial emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage of a benign chronic flare
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a known, reperfusing autoimmune Raynaud flare as a new emergency and escalating to ED/000 rather than managing symptomatically, referring, and safety-netting — the primary scored defect for this case.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring that drops the dangerous mimic
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The opposite error: anchoring on 'just her usual Raynaud' and failing to screen for, or safety-net, genuine critical digital ischaemia or acute arterial occlusion.
Recommending hot water or a heating pad directly on anaesthetic digits, causing a thermal burn the patient cannot feel.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether radial and digital pulses are present (requires palpation)
Capillary refill time and true tissue perfusion
Skin changes of sclerodactyly / telangiectasia on examination
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and oxygen saturation without home devices
Bloods (ANA titre, extractable nuclear antigen panel, inflammatory markers) and any imaging
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage the flare remotely (gentle rewarming, symptomatic relief, cold avoidance), arrange GP review within about 1 week plus rheumatology referral, and give robust, time-bound safety-netting for critical digital ischaemia (urgent ED / 000 if a digit stays white/cold/numb for more than 2 hours despite warming, or a fingertip blackens). Do not reflexively escalate a reperfusing flare to emergency services.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mis-triage in BOTH directions: over-escalating a stable, reperfusing chronic Raynaud flare to ED/000, OR falsely reassuring and dropping the safety-net for genuine critical/irreversible digital ischaemia and acute arterial occlusion, which the patient's own reperfusion (colour returning, warming) currently argues against.
